summary: Hepcidin is the master regulator of iron homeostasis, produced by the liver, and it degrades ferroportin to reduce iron absorption.
Hepcidin is produced by the kidneys and promotes iron absorption by upregulating ferroportin.
Transferrin is the master regulator of iron homeostasis, controlling iron absorption in the duodenum.
Iron is absorbed in the duodenum and proximal jejunum; absorption is enhanced by vitamin C and inhibited by phytates.
Iron is absorbed in the ileum and colon; absorption is enhanced by calcium and inhibited by vitamin C.
Iron is absorbed equally throughout the entire small intestine with no dietary modulators.
Serum ferritin is the best single marker to distinguish iron deficiency anaemia (low ferritin) from anaemia of chronic disease (normal/high ferritin).
Serum iron is the best marker to distinguish IDA from ACD.
Haemoglobin level alone is sufficient to distinguish IDA from ACD.
Transferrin carries two Fe3+ ions per molecule, and TIBC is increased in iron deficiency anaemia.
Transferrin carries one Fe3+ ion per molecule, and TIBC is decreased in iron deficiency.
Ferritin is the transport protein for iron in plasma, carrying four Fe3+ ions.
The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([conjugate base]/[weak acid]).
The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa - log([conjugate base]/[weak acid]).
The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([weak acid]/[conjugate base]).
Metabolic acidosis is characterised by low bicarbonate and compensated by hyperventilation (Kussmaul breathing).
Metabolic acidosis is characterised by high bicarbonate and compensated by hypoventilation.
Metabolic acidosis is always caused by CO2 retention and cannot be compensated.
Normal blood pH is 7.35-7.45; values below 7.35 indicate acidosis, above 7.45 indicate alkalosis.
Normal blood pH is 7.0-7.2; values below 7.0 indicate acidosis.
Normal blood pH is 7.5-7.6; values below 7.5 indicate acidosis.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review minerals, electrolytes, and acid-base balance.
Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
